abstract: In recent years, three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have established themselves as a premier material class for developing high-efficiency, low-cost optoelectronic devices. This momentum has catalyzed interest in various subclasses, most notably two-dimensional (2D) halide perovskites, which have become instrumental in refining our understanding of the structural and chemical physics governing this material family. Although they share a common chemical foundation with their 3D counterparts, the layered architecture of 2D perovskites creates a hybrid organic–inorganic interface that yields unique emergent properties. By exploiting the intrinsic compatibility between these dimensionalities, researchers can develop synergistic heteroarchitectures that mitigate the inherent weaknesses of each individual component. Specifically, integrating 3D and 2D phases can unlock novel behaviors unattainable by either material alone. This review explores how fundamental structural divergences between 3D and 2D perovskites dictate their distinct material profiles and outlines chemical strategies for engineering mixed-dimensional systems via solution-processing. We provide a comprehensive assessment of the impact of 3D–2D architectures on solar cell performance and stability, while also investigating their potential in applications beyond photovoltaics. Ultimately, we offer a perspective on mixed-dimensional perovskites as a highly tunable semiconductor platform capable of delivering the efficiency and durability required for next-generation technologies.
